old_claim.note: Printed Financial Price Lists provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Fire-insurance claims assessment relied on policy registers, endorsements, property descriptions, premiums, and recorded claims or committee minutes rather than printed market price lists.

source_shape.source_locator: The London Archives guide says fire-insurance policy registers record policy number, date, policy holder, property description, premium, and endorsements, and identifies company minutes recording claims against loss.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents policy registers and claim records as the relevant information substrate for fire-insurance claims.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That recordkeeping is directly related to checking coverage and claims, whereas printed financial price lists are market-information artifacts not evidenced as a claims-assessment prerequisite.

ontology_before: Insurance Claims Adjustment depended on printed financial price lists, implying market quotation publication enabled claims settlement.
ontology_after: Early Insurance Claims Assessment depends on record keeping because policy registers, endorsements, and claim minutes are the relevant documented inputs.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: insurance_claims_adjustment now depends on record_keeping and no longer directly depends on printed_financial_price_lists.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened to actuarial market pricing rather than claims assessment.
A source showed early fire claims settlement depended directly on printed financial price lists.
The record_keeping node were later narrowed to exclude institutional policy registers, endorsements, or claims minutes.
short_reason: Claims assessment needs policy and claim records, not printed financial price lists.
